summary: ObjectId representation is not very userfriendly
description:
ObjectIds in the shell are represented as a hex strings:

> ObjectId()
 ObjectId( "4a96ba505fa0beca463e53cd")
> print(ObjectId())
4a96ba775fa0beca463e53cf

In python they are binary strings:

>>> pymongo.objectid.ObjectId()
ObjectId('J\x96\xba5\xe2?k{\x10\x00\x00\x01')
>>> print pymongo.objectid.ObjectId()
J?????k{

If python also used hex it would be easier to switch between the two and it would be easier to understand for a beginner.

I propose that repr and str return the encoded hex instead and that ObjectId() should take either a binary or a hex string as argument.

summary: Cannot create index using: mycollection.ensure_index( {'date': 1} )
description:
The python driver has a bug in:

http://github.com/mongodb/mongo-python-driver/blob/4d517f84503adeee4c8f4924cc6453bbe099a372/pymongo/collection.py#L387

As far as I can tell, this should be: 
        return u"_".join([u"%s_%s" % (item, item) for item in keys])

This fixes the python bug. They may be another bug though. It does not seem possible to create an index using the python driver, but perhaps I'm using it incorrectly.

mycollection.ensure_index( {'date': 1} )

summary: pymongo generates duplicate ObjectId with python multiprocessing
description:
The optimization introduced in http://github.com/mongodb/mongo-python-driver/commit/adeb03c4a530f1a67a272a77339e6ff6762ddf02 broke oid generation when using the multiprocessing module. The following generates duplicate ids:

import os
import time
import pymongo
import multiprocessing

def f(x):
    time.sleep(0.1)
    return "pid=%d, _id=%s" % (os.getpid(), pymongo.objectid.ObjectId())

if __name__ == '__main__':
    pool = multiprocessing.Pool(2)
    for id in pool.map(f, range(10)):
        print id

pid=19233, _id=4ad471b2b072de4b20000000
pid=19233, _id=4ad471b2b072de4b20000001
pid=19234, _id=4ad471b2b072de4b20000000
pid=19234, _id=4ad471b2b072de4b20000001
pid=19233, _id=4ad471b2b072de4b20000002
pid=19233, _id=4ad471b3b072de4b20000003
pid=19234, _id=4ad471b2b072de4b20000002
pid=19234, _id=4ad471b3b072de4b20000003

summary: Memory Leak in the Python driver
description:
Python driver appears to leak memory.

I'm doing a very straightforward load - select everything out of a mysql database and chuck the resulting rows straight into mongo.  I've tried both the single document inserts and bulk inserts, and in each case, I'm running out of memory after about 3 million inserts.  If I comment out the mongo insert, memory remains constant.

These are fairly small records with 24 fields.

summary: float keys crash driver
list(cursor) is too slow
description: I've attached a script to benchmark a few things. Insertion, find_one, and find.next() is just informative -- the latter two are the most important in this case. Iterating over the cursor and append to a list is faster than the simpler "list(cursor)" approach. This is probably a Python bug, but it would be nice to sidestep that by providing a cursor.as_list() function which uses a for loop to make a list.

change update to return if safe=True
description:
Right now, collections.Collection.update  returns None for all cases ( safe = False, and safe = True).
When safe is True, maybe it should return something analogous to insert and save.  I'd lean toward the id's that were touched, but maybe the whole getlasterror result?


14:38 < gregglind> what should an update query return if safe is True... the object?
14:40 <@eliot> either nothing or the # of objects modfied
14:40 <@eliot> or debatably the entire getlasterror object
14:41 < gregglind> how can one tell if an update really "went" uness it returns something?
14:41 <@eliot> well if safe mode is on - then it really went unless an exception is thrown is generally
               the way i think about it
14:42 <@eliot> but returned the # of obejcts touched is decent
14:42 <@eliot> or thats a good reason to use the getlasterror result
14:42 <@eliot> null if safe mode is off
14:42 < gregglind> yeah, the python driver returns none
14:42 <@eliot> otherwise gives you details
14:43 < gregglind> coll.update(dict(_id=collector, lts={"$lt": value}), dict(lts=value),
                   upsert=False,safe=True)
14:44 < gregglind> that is my actual usecase.  I'm trying to update something (given a condition), and I
                   want to know if it changed anything or not
14:44 < gregglind> and "# of objects touched" would probably do it.

summary: Documentation for collection.update is meaningless
description:
Looking at http://api.mongodb.org/python/1.3%2B/api/pymongo/collection.html#pymongo.collection.Collection.update

There is no definition of what an "upsert" actually is.  As far as I can tell it is jargon used by some SQL databases.  (The current docstring is like those crappy manuals telling you that the "enable" switch turns on "enable".  Unless you already know what it actually is, the documentation is useless.)

"manipulate" is in the same boat.  What does it *actually* mean?

"safe" is an incredibly bad name for a parameter (also used elsewhere).  Not only that it defaults to False.  This directly implies to newbies that MongoDB can be either "fast" or "safe" (why else have a choice) and the default of False implies no concern for data integrity!  As far as I can tell "safe" is actually about being asynchronous with you having to check error codes some other way (but how do you tie it to a particular request?)  Why not call it 'async'?

summary: GridFS incorrectly calculates chunk number for streaming reads
description: When reading a file using a `size`  argument that doesn't align evenly with `chunk_size`, the read call will return data from the wrong chunk.

summary: insert returns objectid if list of length one was passed in
description:
The collection.insert method takes a document or a list of documents as the first parameter.  It returns the object id of whataver was inserted.  In theory if you supplied a list then you should get a list of object ids and if you supplied a document then it should return an objectid.

This however breaks down if you supply a list of one item.  In that case an objectid is returned instead of a list of one objectid.

The cause of the bug is because early in the method if one items is supplied then the parameter is turned into a list of one item, and at the end the code assumes that if the list is one item long then it was because a document was supplied.  That assumption is false.

summary: pymongo does not work across forks, bizarre errors
description:
If you have a pymongo connection open, do a fork and then use the connection you get bizarre assertion error messages about ids not matching like this:

  File "build/bdist.linux-x86_64/egg/pymongo/connection.py", line 536, in _send_message_with_response
    return self.__send_and_receive(message, _sock)
  File "build/bdist.linux-x86_64/egg/pymongo/connection.py", line 516, in __send_and_receive
    return self.__receive_message_on_socket(1, request_id, sock)
  File "build/bdist.linux-x86_64/egg/pymongo/connection.py", line 506, in __receive_message_on_socket
    struct.unpack("<i", header[8:12])[0])
AssertionError: ids don't match 1728459718 484616963

Of course the correct answer is not to do this sort of thing.  However I was bitten by two things - one is using the multiprocessing library (standard part of Python) which does fork on Unix and that I had cached connections using threading.local which doesn't discard on a fork.

I suggest doing one or more of the following:

- Document not working across forks
- Do something stronger than the assertion I see (it would have been missed if I started python with -O)
- Store os.getpid() on making the connection and then complain in any request where os.getpid() != stored os.getpid()
- Do the last one, but just auto-reconnect instead of giving an error

summary: create_index, ensure_index: Optional parameter `name`
description:
I'd like to call 'ensure_index' like this:

    db = pymongo.Connection(.fields
    db.fields.ensure_index([("class", pymongo.ASCENDING),
                            ("stream", pymongo.ASCENDING),
                            ("expver", pymongo.ASCENDING),
                            ("number", pymongo.ASCENDING),
                            ("param", pymongo.ASCENDING),
                            ("levtype", pymongo.ASCENDING),
                            ("levelist", pymongo.ASCENDING),
                            ("base_time", pymongo.ASCENDING),
                            ("resolution", pymongo.ASCENDING),
                            ("grid", pymongo.ASCENDING),
                            ("param_alt", pymongo.ASCENDING),
                            ("step", pymongo.ASCENDING),
                            ("valid_time", pymongo.ASCENDING)])

The default index name generated by the Python driver is larger than the 128-character limit. By adding support for an optional `name` parameter, I could specify an index name and the operation would have a chance of suceeding.

I've implemented it here:

    http://github.com/carletes/mongo-python-driver/commit/627f7ae6ef7b39650dcb4efe6c1d4e3c4fb61b47

Any chance of getting this functionallity integrated?

Cheers,

Carlos Valiente <carlos@pepelabs.net>

summary: Add constants to binary module for all binary subtypes
pymongo doesn't support querying on slaves in a replica pair
description:
Take two MongoDB servers in a replica pair - ("localhost", 27017) and ("localhost", 27018). 27017 is the master and 27018 is the slave.  When attempting to connect directly to the slave, we get the following traceback:

>>> conn = pymongo.connection.Connection("localhost", 27018, slave_okay=True)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/Users/michaelr/projects/test_project/lib/python2.6/site-packages/pymongo-1.5-py2.6-macosx-10.3-fat.egg/pymongo/connection.py", line 184, in __init__
    self.__find_master()
  File "/Users/michaelr/projects/test_project/lib/python2.6/site-packages/pymongo-1.5-py2.6-macosx-10.3-fat.egg/pymongo/connection.py", line 456, in __find_master
    ((host, port), master))
pymongo.errors.ConfigurationError: ('localhost', 27018) claims master is (u'localhost', 27017), but that's not configured

Okay, fair enough.

Connecting with a paired connection works fine - but even with the addition of slave_okay to pymongo 1.5 it appears to always query on the master.

It doesn't seem like it's possible, in pymongo, to connect to a slave in a replica pair for querying.

summary: Add a from_datetime() static method to ObjectId
description:
For convenience doing date range constraints on _id in queries


See email below for example:

Right now you'd need to manually create a dummy _id value w/ the right
timestamp and use that to sort on. We could probably add an
ObjectId.from_datetime() method to make that a bit nicer. Mind filing
a JIRA for this?
- Hide quoted text -

On Wed, Mar 31, 2010 at 2:04 PM, Andrew Burrows <burrowsa@gmail.com> wrote:
> Hi
>
> I'm currently developing an application on top of mongo. At the moment
> I have a time stamp field in my documents which allows me to query
> documents created in the last 24 hours and do a group by where I group
> into 5 minute buckets. I've just been reading that the _id field
> contains a time stamp and thought I could just use this and drop my
> extra field. Is this possible? How to I refer to the time stamp part
> of _id in my query?
>
> Here is some example python of the kind of thing I'm doing:
>
> # just a function to get me the time as seconds since epoc
> def ts(dt):
>  return int(mktime(dt.timetuple()))
>
> # here is an insert
> db.box.insert(dict(ts=ts(datetime.now()),  x=randint(0, 500),
> y=randint(0, 100)))
>
> # here is a query
> db.box.group(key       = 'function(obj){ return { ts : 300 *
> parseInt(obj.ts/300) }; }',
>                      condition = dict(ts={'$gt': ts(datetime.now() -
> timedelta(hours=24)) }),
>                      initial   =  dict( xmax=0, ysum=0, count=0 ),
>                      reduce    = 'function(obj,prev) { prev.xmax =
> Math.max(prev.xmax, obj.x); prev.ysum += obj.y; prev.count++; }',
>                      finalize  = 'function(out){ out.yavg =
> out.ysum / out.count }')
>
> Ideally I'd replace it with something like:
>
> # here is an insert - look no ts
> db.box.insert(dict(x=randint(0, 500), y=randint(0, 100)))
>
> # here is a query
> db.box.group(key       = 'function(obj){ return { ts : 300 *
> parseInt(obj._id.timestamp/300) }; }',
>                      condition = {'_id.timestamp' : {'$gt':
> ts(datetime.now() - timedelta(hours=24)) }},
>                      initial   =  dict( xmax=0, ysum=0, count=0 ),
>                      reduce    = 'function(obj,prev) { prev.xmax =
> Math.max(prev.xmax, obj.x); prev.ysum += obj.y; prev.count++; }',
>                      finalize  = 'function(out){ out.yavg =
> out.ysum / out.count }')
>
> Is this kind of thing possible? what property/method do I need to use
> on _id?
>
> Thanks
> Andy
>

summary: seek and tell for GridFile
description: I implemented seek and tell on GridFile. Only supports read-mode because that's all I need at the moment and supporting seek and tell on writable files would require more extensive changes to GridFile. See http://github.com/mikejs/mongo-python-driver/commit/4b503c5d908c7fd5dfea30bed26c0c65ab7f7fc5

summary: GridFS should never allow modification/removal of chunks
Code scope causes segfault
description:
If the scope passed to the code class has an object like a collection it causes a segfault during the db.eval.

This is an example code, 

db.eval(Code("my code", scope={"collection" : my_collection }) 

which is worng because it should be

db.eval(Code("my code", scope={"collection" : my_collection.name })

The thing is that the first code should raise an InvalidDocument instead of going in segfault.

summary: Tuple support in parameter list
description: Is it possible to add Tuple support in parameter? most of parameter which need array only support List.

summary: Year 2038 Problem
description:
For dates after 19 January 2038, 32-bit system integers representing the date (as seconds since 1 January 1970) overflow. Both Python and MongoDB internally handle this correctly, but PyMongo uses the integer representation (specifically the C type time_t) to convert between the two and that produces errors on 32-bit systems. This is not an issue on systems that have a larger time_t type (most if not all 64-bit systems for example).

There are issues with both _cbsonmodule.c and bson.py.

bson.py:

datetime.datetime.utcfromtimestamp() internally uses time_t so can't convert large dates in MongoDB into Python on 32-bit systems. Not sure what the workaround here is (using time.gmtime() does not work). Converting to Mongo works because calendar.timegm returns a long if necessary.

_cbsonmodule.c (my C skills are rusty to say the least so I may well have this wrong):

* Replace the time_t type and related conversion methods to handle epochs > 32-bits. Perhaps something like http://code.google.com/p/y2038/ can help?

summary: negative datetime value in struct
description:
A datetime object with a date of 1940, 12, 31 gives a negative millisecond value.
>>> import datetime
>>> datetime.datetime(1940, 3, 3).timetuple()
(1940, 3, 3, 0, 0, 0, 6, 63, -1)

This ends in bson.py _get_date method with a ValueError:
"timestamp out of range for platform localtime()/gmtime() function"

because of the negative seconds given from the -1 in the datetime.

The solution whould be to change the datetime part in
bson.py _element_to_bson and make sure that the struct value is not negative.

btw, this carshes the hole python process if cbson is used.

summary: index_information returns keys in incorrect order
description:
since the query to get indexes returns a normal dict:

>>> c.ensure_index([('url',-1),('geoglob',-1),('start_date',-1),('end_date',-1)])
u'url_-1_geoglob_-1_start_date_-1_end_date_-1'
>>> c.index_information()
{u'url_-1_geoglob_-1_start_date_-1_end_date_-1': [(u'url', -1), (u'end_date', -1), (u'start_date', -1), (u'geoglob', -1)], u'_id_': [(u'_id', 1)]}
>>>

summary: provide unique status as part of index_information
bulk insert fail with AutoReconnect (pymongo1.6/mongo 1.4.2)
description:
During insert of multiple docs whose individual size close to 4MB limit, the bulk insert fails with AutoReconnect exception. To reproduce a bug, split any text file whose size more then 4MB into chunks, e.g. split -b 3m my_text_file.txt (here size of 1 chunk is 3MB). This will create files: xaa, xab, etc. Then you may use the following stand-alone code with xaa file to reproduce the problem

import sys
from pymongo import Connection

db = Connection()
coll = db['db']['test']

data = open('xaa', 'r').read()
print "Size of data", sys.getsizeof(data)
rec = {'key1':data}
coll.remove({})
coll.insert(rec)
print "Number of docs:", coll.find().count()

coll.remove({})
docs = [{'rec%s' % k: dict(rec)} for k in range(0,10)]
for rec in docs:
    coll.insert(rec)
print "Number of docs:", coll.find().count()

coll.remove({})
coll.insert(docs)
print "Number of docs:", coll.find().count()

This code inserts all 10 docs one by one, while raise the following exception during bulk insert:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test_bulk.py", line 23, in <module>
    coll.insert(docs)
  File "/opt/local/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.6/lib/python2.6/site-packages/pymongo/collection.py", line 245, in insert
    message.insert(self.__full_name, docs, check_keys, safe), safe)
  File "/opt/local/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.6/lib/python2.6/site-packages/pymongo/connection.py", line 574, in _send_message
    raise AutoReconnect(str(e))
pymongo.errors.AutoReconnect: [Errno 32] Broken pipe
